ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Korean marinated beef short ribs with cheese. FOODON_03302051 (beef short rib food product) is the closest parent. The cheese topping and Korean marinade are not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Beef, ribs) is the closest parent. The Korean marinade, cheese topping, and composite dish nature are not captured. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this dish.

serving.description: 1 plate (beef kalbi with cheese, rice, and side dishes)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of beef kalbi with cheese consists of approximately 450 g, including marinated beef short ribs, melted cheese topping, a portion of steamed white rice, and assorted Korean side dishes (banchan).

nutrient_source.title: Beef short ribs, cooked, braised (USDA SR Legacy, NDB 13352) combined with component estimation for Korean soy-sugar marinade and mozzarella cheese (USDA FoodData Central)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Korean beef kalbi with cheese as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked beef short ribs (NDB 13352, braised), USDA FoodData Central data for part-skim mozzarella cheese, and standard Korean soy-sugar-sesame marinade component data. Steamed white rice contribution is included in the per-100g composite estimate. Proportions estimated as approximately 45% beef short rib (cooked), 10% cheese, 30% steamed rice, 15% marinade and side dishes by weight. This is a medium-confidence composite estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021; analogous estimate based on mixed rice-and-meat dish (white rice GI ~72, blended with protein and fat components reducing effective GI to approximately 54 (low GI category) for the composite dish)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed journal (American Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean beef kalbi with cheese. GI estimated at 54 (low) based on the composite dish composition: steamed white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) blended with substantial protein (beef, cheese) and fat components, which are well-established to attenuate postprandial glycemic response. The soy-sugar marinade contributes additional simple sugars. The blended GI of approximately 54 is consistent with published analogous mixed rice-meat dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 72 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving) / 100 ≈ 38.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-based marinade and processed cheese
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 2160 mg per full serving), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single meal; requesting reduced-marinade or sauce-on-the-side preparations may help lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index composite dish but with a high glycemic load from significant carbohydrate content (white rice, sugar-containing marinade)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that this dish carries a low glycemic index (estimated GI 54) but a high glycemic load per serving (approximately 38.9) driven by the white rice component and sugar in the marinade; portion control of the rice component and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables from the banchan side dishes can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef short ribs and cheese, combined with high sodium from marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation due to its saturated fat content (estimated 3.8 g per 100 g, approximately 17 g per serving) from beef short ribs and mozzarella cheese, and its high sodium content from the soy-based marinade, both of which are associated with elevated LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk; choosing leaner cuts or reducing portion size is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Kalbi with Cheese (치즈 소갈비, Chijeu Sogalbi) is a Korean restaurant dish consisting of marinated beef short ribs (galbi) that have been steeped in a traditional Korean house marinade — typically a blend of soy sauce, sugar, garlic, sesame oil, ginger, and Asian pear or apple for tenderising — then cooked in a wok to achieve a caramelized, smoky exterior. The dish is finished with melted cheese, most commonly mozzarella, which adds a creamy, mild richness that contrasts with the savoury-sweet depth of the marinade. It is served as a complete meal with steamed white rice and an assortment of Korean side dishes (banchan), which may include kimchi, pickled vegetables, and seasoned spinach. The texture of the beef is tender and slightly charred at the edges, with a glossy, caramelized glaze from the sugar in the marinade. The cheese topping introduces a Western fusion element that has become popular in contemporary Korean cuisine and Korean-influenced restaurants across Southeast Asia, including Singapore. Nutritionally, this is a protein-rich, moderate-fat dish with significant carbohydrate contribution from the rice component. The sodium content is elevated due to the soy-based marinade and cheese. The dish has a low glycemic index (estimated GI 54) as a composite, though the glycemic load per full serving is high due to the rice portion. Clinically, individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or Type 2 Diabetes should exercise portion control, particularly regarding the rice and marinade components. The dish provides meaningful amounts of zinc, vitamin B12, iron, and calcium from the beef and cheese components.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Korean beef kalbi with cheese as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy and USDA FoodData Central data for the component ingredients (beef short ribs, mozzarella cheese, steamed white rice, soy-sugar marinade) with proportional weighting. The GI is an analogous estimate based on the composite dish composition using University of Sydney GI Database reference values for white rice and published mixed rice-meat dish analogues. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and grounded in approved sources, but no direct measurement of this specific dish exists in any approved primary database.
notes: 1. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Dairy and eggs', which is incorrect for a beef-dominant dish. Corrected to 'Meat and alternatives' based on the primary ingredient being beef short ribs. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite dish in the USDA FoodData Central component sources. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite dish in the USDA FoodData Central component sources. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported with sufficient confidence for this composite dish estimate. 5. The GI of 54 is an analogous estimate, falling into the 'low' glycemic category; no direct GI measurement for Korean beef kalbi with cheese exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 6. The is_halal flag is set to false because the dish contains soy-based marinade ingredients and is served at a restaurant (Ajumma's - Funan, Singapore) where halal certification status is not confirmed in the input data. 7. Serving size of 450 g is an estimate for a full restaurant plate including rice and side dishes; the beef component alone is estimated at approximately 200 g cooked weight. 8. The input food_name 'Signature Beef Kalbi with Cheese' has been canonicalized to 'Beef Kalbi with Cheese' by removing the promotional qualifier 'Signature'. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and carry medium confidence.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record. Identified food_group error in input (Dairy and eggs → Meat and alternatives). Canonicalized food_name by removing promotional qualifier. Added Korean local name 치즈 소갈비. Constructed composite nutrient estimate from USDA FoodData Central approved sources. Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) in notes. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list.

Iteration 2: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for glycemic_index 54, as per GI classification rules (GI <= 55 is low). Updated health_context.type2_diabetes key_factor and guidance, llm_training.natural_language_description, and gi_source.source_match_notes to reflect the corrected low GI category while maintaining 'moderate' concern level due to high glycemic load. Updated notes and veritas_qc sections accordingly.
